content_en:
No theme runs more consistently through al-Shawkani's scholarship than his critique of taqlid — the practice of following the rulings of a legal school without examining the underlying evidence for those rulings. In Irshad al-Fuhul and across his other works, including his dedicated treatise al-Qaul al-Mufid fi Adillat al-Ijtihad wal-Taqlid, al-Shawkani develops one of the most thorough and pointed arguments against obligatory school-following in the classical literature.

Al-Shawkani's starting point is definitional. He distinguishes between two categories of Muslims: those who have no capacity for independent legal reasoning (the layperson, the general public), and those who possess the qualifications of a mujtahid — comprehensive knowledge of the Quran, the Sunnah, the tools of Arabic linguistics, the positions of the scholars, and the principles of legal reasoning. For the first group, taqlid is permissible and indeed necessary: they have no alternative but to ask a scholar. Al-Shawkani does not dispute this. His target is the second group: scholars who have mastered the tools of ijtihad but decline to use them, deferring instead to the conclusions of a classical imam (Abu Hanifa, Malik, al-Shafi'i, or Ahmad ibn Hanbal) even when they encounter clear evidence that points in a different direction.

This practice, al-Shawkani argues, represents a confusion of two distinct things: revering the great imams for their learning and piety, which is entirely appropriate, and treating their conclusions as if they carry the same authority as the Quran and the authentic Sunnah, which is not. The classical imams themselves, he notes repeatedly, explicitly instructed their students to leave their positions when a sound hadith contradicted them. Al-Shafi'i's famous statement — 'If a hadith is authentic, that is my position, and throw my saying against the wall' — is cited by al-Shawkani as direct evidence that the imams never intended their rulings to function as a final barrier between the scholar and the primary sources.

Al-Shawkani's critique is sharpened by his treatment of concrete cases where madhab positions diverged from what he regarded as well-authenticated hadith evidence. He was particularly concerned with situations where scholars acknowledged the existence of a relevant hadith but continued to follow their school's ruling on the grounds that the imam must have had a reason for departing from it — even when that reason was unknown. For al-Shawkani, this amounts to treating the imam's judgment as infallible: a position that no imam ever claimed for himself and that has no basis in the sources.

The traditionalist response to al-Shawkani's position was substantial. Critics argued that following an established school provides coherence and prevents the chaos of every scholar adopting their own synthesis of the primary sources. They pointed out that the conditions al-Shawkani set for ijtihad are extremely demanding and that very few scholars in later centuries actually meet them. Al-Shawkani engaged these objections directly. He acknowledged that unqualified ijtihad is worse than disciplined taqlid, but insisted that the solution to bad ijtihad is better training — not a categorical prohibition on independent reasoning that the early generations never endorsed.

His position had practical implications for the Yemeni context in which he worked. As a senior judge, al-Shawkani used his platform to encourage direct engagement with hadith literature in legal reasoning, setting a model that influenced subsequent generations of Yemeni scholars and contributed to Yemen's distinctive tradition of rigorous hadith-based jurisprudence.
content_id:
Tidak ada tema yang lebih konsisten mengalir melalui beasiswa al-Syawkani daripada kritiknya terhadap taqlid — praktik mengikuti putusan-putusan madzhab hukum tanpa memeriksa bukti yang mendasarinya. Dalam Irsyad al-Fuhul dan di seluruh karya-karyanya yang lain, termasuk risalah khususnya Al-Qaul al-Mufid fi Adillat al-Ijtihad wat-Taqlid, al-Syawkani mengembangkan salah satu argumen paling menyeluruh dan tajam terhadap mengikuti madzhab secara wajib dalam literatur klasik.

Titik awal al-Syawkani bersifat definitif. Beliau membedakan antara dua kategori Muslim: mereka yang tidak memiliki kapasitas untuk penalaran hukum independen (orang awam, masyarakat umum), dan mereka yang memiliki kualifikasi seorang mujtahid — pengetahuan komprehensif tentang Al-Qur'an, Sunnah, alat-alat linguistik Arab, posisi-posisi ulama, dan prinsip-prinsip penalaran hukum. Untuk kelompok pertama, taqlid diperbolehkan dan bahkan diperlukan: mereka tidak memiliki pilihan selain bertanya kepada seorang ulama. Al-Syawkani tidak memperdebatkan ini. Sasarannya adalah kelompok kedua: ulama-ulama yang telah menguasai alat-alat ijtihad namun menolak menggunakannya, mendefer sebaliknya kepada kesimpulan-kesimpulan seorang imam klasik (Abu Hanifah, Malik, al-Syafi'i, atau Ahmad ibn Hanbal) bahkan ketika mereka menemukan bukti yang jelas menunjuk ke arah yang berbeda.

Praktik ini, al-Syawkani berargumen, merupakan kebingungan antara dua hal yang berbeda: menghormati para imam besar karena keilmuan dan ketakwaan mereka, yang sepenuhnya tepat, dan memperlakukan kesimpulan-kesimpulan mereka seolah-olah membawa otoritas yang sama dengan Al-Qur'an dan Sunnah yang autentik, yang tidak. Para imam klasik itu sendiri, beliau berulang kali mencatat, secara eksplisit memerintahkan murid-murid mereka untuk meninggalkan posisi-posisi mereka ketika sebuah hadis yang shahih bertentangan dengan mereka. Pernyataan al-Syafi'i yang terkenal — 'Jika sebuah hadis itu shahih, itulah pandanganku, dan lemparkan perkataanku ke dinding' — dikutip oleh al-Syawkani sebagai bukti langsung bahwa para imam tidak pernah bermaksud putusan-putusan mereka berfungsi sebagai pengganti penalaran independen dari teks-teks.

Kritik al-Syawkani terhadap taqlid bukan merupakan penolakan terhadap tradisi atau warisan ilmiah Islam. Sebaliknya, ini adalah argumen bahwa kesetiaan sejati kepada warisan itu — kepada para imam yang pendapatnya begitu dihormati — mengharuskan mengikuti metode mereka dan tidak sekadar kesimpulan mereka. Metode para imam adalah ijtihad langsung dari Al-Qur'an dan Sunnah. Dengan menolak untuk melakukan ijtihad dan sekadar mengikuti kesimpulan, seorang ulama modern justru mengkhianati semangat tradisi yang diklaim untuk dihormatinya. Argumen ini, yang diajukan dengan kekuatan besar dan contoh yang melimpah dalam Irsyad al-Fuhul, terus membentuk debat-debat Islam tentang otoritas hukum dan ijtihad.
